findings.message: This success fixture no longer exercises the spec-coverage pass path: because `task-coverage.json` is absent, `VerifyCommand::SpecCoverage` returns via the new skip guard before any coverage evaluation runs. The test will still pass if `evaluate_coverage()` regresses, so it no longer protects the covered-track CLI behavior.

`execute_next_task` and `execute_task_counts` now bypass the track store entirely and return success with `null`/`0` payloads for any syntactically valid track id. That makes a missing or corrupt track indistinguishable from a real empty track, so `sotp track next-task` and `task-counts` will silently report 'no work' instead of failing closed while T007 is pending.

`execute_next_task` and `execute_task_counts` now ignore `items_dir` entirely and return success for any syntactically valid track id, including tracks that do not exist. That is not a fail-safe T007 stub: callers of `sotp track next-task` get a null task and callers of `sotp track task-counts` get all zeros instead of the previous metadata-read error, which can silently hide a bad `--items-dir` or missing/corrupt track in automation. Keep the TODO stubbed task data if needed, but still load/validate the track metadata first and propagate read/not-found errors.

`next-task` and `task-counts` now return successful, schema-valid empty results while the real task data is unavailable pending T007. A caller cannot distinguish this stub from a track with no open tasks or zero tasks, so automation can silently make decisions from false data. Since the briefing requires T007 stubs to be fail-safe, these commands should either read `impl-plan.json` or fail closed with a clear T007-pending error instead of printing null/zero JSON with `ExitCode::SUCCESS`.

Both `next-task` and `task-counts` have changed from the documented JSON payloads to ad hoc text output (`"<id> — <description>"` and `total=... resolved=... unresolved=...`). That breaks the machine-readable CLI contract consumed by the existing wrappers/specs and removes the per-status counts callers expect.

The integration tests still miss the required `done -> in_progress` leg of the transition round-trip. `transition_subcommand_persists_commit_hash_on_done_transition` only covers `todo -> in_progress -> done`, so a regression in reopening a completed task would not be caught.

`bin/sotp track next-task` now formats the JSON payload with raw string interpolation instead of serializing it. A task description containing `"`, `\`, or a newline will emit invalid JSON and break the command's documented machine-readable contract.

`bin/sotp track resolve` still special-cases branchless schema v3 tracks, but dropping the raw `status == planned` check means a corrupted archived track whose task list still looks planned will now pass as `Planned` and be told to activate. That removes the fail-closed guard for frozen v3 metadata.

Using `metadata.status()` alone here lets schema v3 archived tracks with unresolved tasks be misclassified as active, so the pre-commit hook can recompute TDDD outputs on frozen content instead of skipping it. The same regression is also present in the sibling `graph.rs`, `contract_map.rs`, and `tddd/signals.rs` guards.

For branchless schema_version 3 tracks, this guard now trusts `TrackMetadata::status()` only. Legacy task state is no longer consulted, so a v3 document that still decodes as `planned` can now pass `resolve` even if its old task state would have been rejected as corrupted. That weakens the fail-closed behavior this command documents.

The rejected-transition case no longer asserts that `impl-plan.json` stays unchanged after a failure. With task state now persisted in a separate file, a partial-write regression on an invalid transition would slip through this test.

`execute_transition` only persists `impl-plan.json` and prints the `TrackMetadata` returned by the use case, but it never writes the new status back to `metadata.json`. After any todo→in_progress/done transition, the explicit status readers added in this change (`resolve`, `type-signals`, `type-graph`, `contract-map`, and the pre-commit hook) will keep seeing the old `planned` value, so active/frozen-track checks and phase resolution become stale.

`execute_transition` never rejects `TrackStatus::Archived`, so a frozen archived track under `track/items` can still be transitioned and have `metadata.json` rewritten. That bypasses the frozen-track invariant enforced elsewhere and can silently unarchive archived tracks.

The branchless-v3 guard now only inspects the explicit `status` field. Legacy v3 metadata whose raw status is `planned` but whose task state is already non-planned will now pass `resolve`, whereas the previous derived-status check failed closed on that corruption.

`derive_track_status_from_impl_plan` does not match the status-sync logic used by `TransitionTaskUseCase`: this helper returns `InProgress` whenever any task is done/skipped, while the use case treats a mixed `todo` + `done/skipped` plan as `Planned` unless some task is actually `in_progress`. After a transition on a multi-task track, the CLI will overwrite `metadata.json` with the wrong track status, which then affects resolve/phase checks.

Clearing an override still delegates to the override use case without loading impl-plan.json to restore the underlying task-derived status. With status now stored explicitly in metadata.json, `bin/sotp track clear-override` on a blocked/cancelled in-progress or done track will persist the track as planned instead of deriving in_progress/done from impl-plan.json, so resolve/registry output reports the wrong phase after the override is cleared.

`clear-override` now performs two separate metadata writes: `SetOverrideUseCase::execute(&track_id, None)` clears the override and sets status to `Planned`, then a second `store.update(...)` re-derives the real status from `impl-plan.json`. If the impl-plan read or the second update fails, the command returns an error after already mutating `metadata.json`, leaving the track in a partially-updated state instead of rolling back or updating atomically.

This branchless-v3 guard no longer consults task state, so a legacy or corrupted `metadata.json` whose explicit `status` is still `planned` but whose tasks are already in progress/done will now resolve instead of being rejected. That drops the old fail-closed check for this code path.

The same explicit-status-only guard now lets `type-signals` run on a frozen legacy track if the stored status is stale `planned`; the previous task-derived check is gone here, and the same pattern is repeated in `contract_map.rs` and `graph.rs`.

The tests split the required round-trip across two cases instead of running `todo -> in_progress -> done --commit-hash -> in_progress` end to end. That means a regression that loses or corrupts the traced commit hash on reopen would still pass this suite.

`next-task` and `task-counts` now treat any missing `impl-plan.json` as an empty/planning-only track. On a migrated `in_progress`/`done` track whose plan file is missing or unreadable, the CLI will silently print null/zero results instead of failing, which masks corruption and can mislead automation into thinking there is no work.

`transition_subcommand_full_round_trip_with_commit_hash_and_reopen` never asserts the post-reopen `commit_hash` state. A regression that incorrectly preserves or drops the hash on `DoneTraced -> InProgress` would still pass because the only post-reopen check is that the file contains `in_progress`.

The shared test fixture still writes schema_version 3 metadata with embedded tasks/plan and no impl-plan.json, while the updated test is meant to verify the schema_version 4 identity-only behavior. This leaves the T005 fixture migration untested for `track signals` and can mask regressions in the new metadata shape.

These contract-map tests still use inline schema_version 3 metadata with embedded tasks/plan and no separate impl-plan.json. The review brief requires in-scope fixtures to use v4 identity-only metadata plus impl-plan.json, so these tests continue exercising legacy metadata instead of the migrated CLI shape.

The type-graph test fixtures still embed tasks/plan in schema_version 3 metadata instead of using v4 identity-only metadata with a separate impl-plan.json. That misses the required fixture migration and leaves this CLI path covered only through the legacy decoder shape.

The type-signals fixture helper still emits schema_version 3 metadata containing tasks/plan and no impl-plan.json. Because many tests use this helper, the updated active-track guard is not being exercised against the required v4 identity-only metadata plus impl-plan fixture shape.

`next-task` and `task-counts` treat a missing `impl-plan.json` as a successful empty plan for every track. The code only comments that this is for a planning-only track, but it never checks the loaded metadata's branch/status before returning null/zero counts. On an activated or in-progress track with a missing/corrupt task store, `bin/sotp track next-task` will incorrectly report no open task and `bin/sotp track task-counts` will report all zeros, silently hiding the task data loss instead of failing closed.

`execute_plan_artifact_refs` turns every unresolved auto-detect case into `VerifyOutcome::pass()`. On `main`, detached HEAD, a non-`track/*` branch, git failure, or when the repo is invoked from a nested working directory, the command will silently succeed instead of surfacing the required finding and failing closed, which hides missing plan-artifact coverage.

`resolve_active_track_dir()` only accepts `track/*` branches. The existing branch-resolution helper and the design intent both treat `plan/*` as a valid active branch for track lookup, so `bin/sotp verify plan-artifact-refs` will incorrectly fail on plan checkouts even when the track directory exists.

The branch lookup resolves `track/items/<id>` relative to the process CWD and never anchors to the repository root. Running `bin/sotp verify plan-artifact-refs` from a subdirectory inside the repo will look for the wrong relative path and emit a false failure on valid `track/*` branches.

No tests were added for the new `PlanArtifactRefs` CLI path, so the clap wiring, explicit `--track-dir` handling, and implicit branch-resolution fallback are all unverified. This is a new public verify entrypoint, so the missing happy-path and error-path coverage is a regression risk.

The new omitted-`--track-dir` branch-resolution path is not exercised by tests. The added tests only cover Clap parsing and explicit-dir success/failure, so regressions in `resolve_active_track_dir()` or its fallback error path would slip through even though this is the core new behavior.

Schema 4 tracks cannot be activated: T005 now writes identity-only metadata with schema_version 4, and execute_activate passes that DocumentMeta schema_version into ActivateTrackUseCase, but this guard still rejects anything other than 3 before materializing the branch. New branchless schema 4 tracks will fail /track:activate with TrackActivationRequiresSchemaV3 even though schema 4 is the new metadata shape.

`ActivateTrackUseCase::execute` still rejects every schema version except 3, but T005 now writes identity-only metadata as schema_version 4. A newly created branchless v4 track that is otherwise planned will fail activation with `TrackActivationRequiresSchemaV3`, so the non-task activation path is broken by this update.

Clearing an override always writes `TrackStatus::Planned`. Because T005 makes track status an explicitly persisted field, clearing a blocked/cancelled override on an active or done track rewrites the stored workflow status to planned instead of restoring or preserving the underlying status, corrupting the track phase for non-task override flows.

The new transition/add-task path performs a separate `find` / `load_impl_plan` / `save_impl_plan` read-modify-write without the per-track lock that `TrackWriter::update` used to provide. Two concurrent `track-add-task` or `track-transition` calls can read the same snapshot, compute the same next `TaskId`, and then the last save wins, dropping one update. This is a data-loss regression and needs a serialized update boundary around the impl-plan mutation.

On a `track/` branch, `BlobFetchResult::NotFound` means the PR head does not contain `impl-plan.json`; `plan/` branches already short-circuit before this point. Returning `VerifyOutcome::pass()` here fail-opens the merge-time task-completion gate when there is no implementation plan to inspect, even though final merge requires `impl-plan.json` task state to be present and resolved.

An empty `ImplPlanDocument` is accepted because `all_tasks_resolved()` is vacuously true, so a PR can pass the merge task-completion gate with `impl-plan.json` present but containing zero tasks. The workflow requirement explicitly blocks empty task sets to avoid this nullable bypass; this use case needs to reject `impl_plan.tasks().is_empty()` before returning pass.

The branchless implementation-transition guard still only rejects `schema_version == 3`. New identity-only tracks are saved as schema version 4 and activation was updated to accept v4, so a branchless v4 planned track can run an implementation transition (`in_progress`, `done`, or `skipped`) without going through activation or materializing a track branch.

`execute_by_status` mutates and saves only `impl-plan.json`, then returns the `TrackMetadata` read before the transition. Since `metadata.json` still carries the explicit persisted track status and no other task-transition path updates it, moving tasks to `in_progress` or `done` leaves `metadata.status` stuck at its old value, so resolve/registry/CLI status consumers continue to treat the track as planned after task state changed.

`AddTaskUseCase::execute` never re-derives or persists `TrackMetadata.status` after appending a new Todo task. If the track was previously `Done`, `metadata.json` stays `done` even though `impl-plan.json` now contains unresolved work, so `resolve_phase()`/CLI status reporting will keep treating the track as ready to ship.

The transition paths write `impl-plan.json` first and then perform a second `metadata.json` update. If that second write fails, the task transition is already committed but the use case returns an error, leaving the two persisted documents out of sync and making retries non-idempotent. The same pattern exists in both `execute` and `execute_by_status`.

`AddTaskUseCase` mutates `impl-plan.json` but never re-syncs `TrackMetadata.status` after inserting a new task. That leaves the persisted metadata and the returned `TrackMetadata` stale for any track that was already `Done` or otherwise not `Planned`: adding a new `Todo` task will still report the old phase, so CLI output and stored metadata can become inconsistent with the updated task list.

AddTaskUseCase::execute persists `impl-plan.json` but never re-derives or writes `metadata.status`. Adding a task to a completed or in-progress track leaves `metadata.json` stale, so later phase checks and CLI output keep reporting the old status even though the track now has an unresolved task.

TransitionTaskUseCase::execute and execute_by_status now save `impl-plan.json` and then update `metadata.json` in a second step. If the metadata update fails after the plan save succeeds, the method returns an error but the repository is left half-updated (impl-plan changed, metadata stale), which reintroduces a data-consistency failure mode the old single-write path avoided.

`AddTaskUseCase` persists the new `impl-plan.json` entry but never re-derives or writes back `metadata.json.status`. Adding a Todo task to a completed track can therefore leave the track marked `done` even though the aggregate is no longer fully resolved.

Both transition paths save `impl-plan.json` before syncing `metadata.json`. If the later `TrackWriter::update` fails, the method returns an error after already mutating the plan, leaving `impl-plan.json` and `metadata.json` out of sync.

`derive_track_status_from_impl_plan` only promotes a track to `InProgress` when a task is already `InProgress`. That leaves partially completed plans with only `done` + `todo` tasks classified as `Planned`, so the transition/add-task use cases can write back the wrong phase and `track_phase` will report Planning even though work is still open. The sync should treat any unresolved task mix as `InProgress`, not just explicit in-progress tasks.
The read-only sandbox prevents me from inspecting `tmp/reviewer-runtime/briefing-usecase.md` or running `git diff`, so I cannot perform an evidence-based review in this environment.

`derive_track_status_from_impl_plan` classifies a mixed plan such as `[done, todo]` as `Planned` because it only returns `InProgress` when a task is explicitly `InProgress`, and otherwise falls through unless all tasks are resolved. That regresses the previous track-status semantics where any non-empty plan with some progress but unresolved work is active; after completing one task in a multi-task track, the use case can write `metadata.status = planned`, causing phase/status consumers to misreport active implementation work.

`convention_refs` should not participate in phase-1 signal evaluation per ADR 2026-04-19-1242 §D3.1. As written, a requirement with only `convention_refs` is classified as Blue, which suppresses the Red finding that `check_spec_doc_signals()` is meant to surface for missing ADR grounding.

`convention_refs` are treated as a Blue signal here, but ADR 2026-04-19-1242 D3.1 says the spec signal must depend only on `adr_refs` and `informal_grounds`; `convention_refs` are excluded. As written, a requirement with only convention refs will be classified as Blue instead of Red, which weakens the gate.

`check_spec_doc_signals()` tells callers to upgrade Yellow by referencing either `adr_refs[]` or `convention_refs[]`, but convention refs are explicitly outside signal evaluation and do not change a requirement from Yellow to Blue. A requirement with only convention refs still evaluates Red, so this guidance is functionally wrong and will mislead users of the gate output.

`TaskCoverageDocument::new` only checks for duplicate `SpecElementId` keys across sections; it never rejects duplicate `TaskId` entries inside a single element’s `Vec<TaskId>`, even though this type advertises that as an enforced invariant. That means callers can still construct coverage documents in an illegal state, and the codec will accept that state unchanged.

`TrackMetadata::with_branch` now only validates the title; it no longer checks that a provided branch matches the track id, and `set_branch` can later introduce the same mismatch. That allows persisted states like `id = my-track` with `branch = track/other-track`, which violates the branch-id consistency required by the new identity-only metadata model and can misroute branch-based readers/activation logic.

`set_branch` bypasses the new branch/id invariant enforced in `TrackMetadata::with_branch`. A caller can mutate an already-validated track into a mismatched `track/<other>` branch, and `ActivateTrackUseCase` uses this setter directly, so the domain can persist an invalid branch/id pair without any error.

`TrackMetadata` no longer enforces coherence between `status` and `status_override`. `new`/`with_branch` accept any combination, and `set_status`/`set_status_override` can mutate a track into `status = Planned` while keeping a blocked/cancelled override. `resolve_phase()` only consults `status`, so the override is then silently ignored and the track is misclassified. The previous implementation prevented impossible states here.

`add_task` is not fail-safe: it appends the new task before validating the target section, and it `mem::take`s `self.plan` before the same validation. Any `SectionNotFound`/`NoSectionsAvailable` error leaves the aggregate mutated and the plan dropped, so a failed call corrupts in-memory state.

`next_num + 1` overflows when the highest existing task suffix is `u64::MAX`. That is a reachable `TaskId` value, so `add_task` can panic in debug builds or wrap to a duplicate `T000` in release builds.

`resolve_transition` only looks at `TaskStatusKind::Done`, so it cannot distinguish `DonePending` from `DoneTraced`. A traced task given target `done` plus a commit hash is mapped to `BackfillHash` and then rejected by `TrackTask::transition`, instead of being classified as an unsupported target.

`add_task` mutates `self.tasks` and `self.plan` before validating `section_id`. If the section is missing or there are no sections, the method returns an error after the new task has already been pushed and `mem::take(&mut self.plan)` has already cleared the plan, leaving the document corrupted on a recoverable error.

The next task ID is computed with `+ 1` on the maximum numeric suffix. When the highest existing task is `T18446744073709551615`, this overflows and panics in library code instead of returning a validation error, regressing the previous checked-add behavior.

`next_open_task` now scans `self.tasks` in storage order and returns the first Todo/InProgress task it encounters. The previous implementation walked plan order and preferred InProgress over Todo, so a task list that is not aligned with the plan can now report the wrong "next" task.

`apply_transition_by_status` collapses `DonePending` and `DoneTraced` into `TaskStatusKind::Done` before resolving `done`. That means a `done` request with a commit hash is always mapped to `BackfillHash`, which `TrackTask::transition` rejects for already-traced tasks. The status-based API therefore cannot distinguish the two done states and fails on an already-traced task instead of choosing the correct transition.

`resolve_phase` and `resolve_phase_from_record` still treat only `schema_version == 3` as branchless/activatable. That means v4 identity-only tracks are classified as `Planning` and the CLI will suggest `/track:implement` instead of `/track:activate`, even though the activation use case already accepts schema v4.

`next_open_task` looks for any `InProgress` task using `self.tasks.iter().find(...)`, so the chosen task depends on storage order rather than plan order. If more than one task is open, `track next-task` can return a later task instead of the earliest one in the plan, which contradicts the method's own ordering contract.
`PlanSection` is used in `add_task` but never imported into this module, so the new `ImplPlanDocument::add_task` implementation does not compile.

`add_task` now maps task-id overflow to `ValidationError::DuplicateTaskId("task id counter overflow")`. That is the wrong error class for an arithmetic overflow and is a behavioral regression from the previous `InvalidTaskId` boundary handling, so callers can no longer distinguish overflow from a real duplicate-id case.

`with_refs` stores `spec_refs` verbatim and never checks whether the same `SpecRef.anchor` appears more than once in a single catalogue entry. That leaves the new structured-ref model able to encode duplicate `SpecElementId` citations without any domain validation, which is the invariant the briefing calls out as needing a new error path.

`all_tasks_resolved()` is vacuously true for an empty `ImplPlanDocument`, so a zero-task `impl-plan.json` is treated as fully complete by downstream checks. That lets an empty implementation-plan file satisfy the completion gate instead of being rejected as missing/incomplete work.
`adr-editor` is referenced in the new `/track:plan` back-and-forth flow, but `.harness/config/agent-profiles.json` never declares that capability, so the routing target cannot be resolved.

`/adr:add` still templates `## Status` with `Accepted | Proposed`, but `knowledge/conventions/pre-track-adr-authoring.md` explicitly forbids `Status`/`approved` fields. The command will generate ADRs that violate the convention it is meant to enforce.

`type-designer.md` still requires `approved: true` on every type entry, which keeps an approval state in the catalogue schema even though the new workflow convention removes ceremonial approval/status fields.

`/track:design` still lists only 12 `TypeDefinitionKind` variants and omits `secondary_adapter`, while `type-designer.md` now treats the taxonomy as 13 variants. The command and writer no longer agree on the accepted catalogue schema.

`/track:plan` now routes ADR back-and-forth to `capabilities.adr-editor`, but this repo's capability map does not define an `adr-editor` entry. As written, the new ADR escalation path cannot be resolved, so Phase 1 🔴 cannot actually re-invoke the writer it names.

`/adr:add` still requires a `## Status` section with `Accepted` / `Proposed`, but the new pre-track ADR convention explicitly forbids ADR status fields and the ceremony-minimization convention removes artificial `Status` / `approved` state. ADRs created by this command would violate the policy the rest of the refactor is introducing.

`/track:design` still hard-codes 12 `TypeDefinitionKind` variants and never mentions `secondary_adapter`, while the new `type-designer` agent treats the taxonomy as 13 variants. Phase 2 will not be able to describe or validate `secondary_adapter` entries, so the command and writer now disagree on the accepted catalogue schema.

`adr-editor` is now part of the orchestration flow, but the agent index never adds an `adr-editor` entry or file the way it does for `spec-designer`, `impl-planner`, and `type-designer`. That leaves the ADR repair loop in `track/plan.md` and `08-orchestration.md` without a custom prompt contract or model pin, so the new back-and-forth path is under-specified.

The language-convention paragraph still cites `## Status` as an example of a valid English section header, but this file and `pre-track-adr-authoring.md` now explicitly remove ADR status fields. That stale example conflicts with the new no-`Status` workflow and can cause freshly authored ADRs to reintroduce the forbidden section.

The new 13-kind list includes `secondary_adapter`, but the context step still tells readers to find the taxonomy by grepping `tddd-taxonomy`. That grep only surfaces the older 12-kind ADR, so the instructions for the new kind are never actually loaded.

This command now says new ADRs must omit `## Status`, but it still tells the agent to read `knowledge/adr/README.md` as the ADR template. The README template still requires `Status`, so a straightforward read/clone path produces ADRs that violate this command's own convention.

The new `secondary_adapter` kind is introduced earlier in the command, but the canonical declaration checklist still only enumerates `expected_variants`, `transitions_to`, and `expected_methods`, and it explicitly says struct-only variants carry no extra fields. That omits `implements`, so `/track:design` can produce incomplete `secondary_adapter` entries that fail downstream validation.

The new workflow policy says artificial approval state is removed, but `/track:design` still instructs the type-designer to emit `approved: true`, preserve existing `approved` status, and iterate until approved. That keeps generating the approval field this change says is no longer part of track artifacts, leaving the harness-policy docs internally inconsistent.

Phase 2 is now documented elsewhere as consuming the Phase 1 `spec.json`, and `type-designer.md` declares `track/items/<id>/spec.json` as an input, but `/track:design` still tells the command to read `spec.md` or fall back to `plan.md` instead. That fallback allows type catalogue authoring to proceed without the spec SSoT and can produce entries without reliable `SpecRef` coverage, contradicting the new SoT Chain.

The custom Opus-pinned agent list omits the new `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` even though `agent-profiles.json`, `/track:plan`, and the agent README register `adr-editor` as a Claude subagent. This leaves the ADR back-and-forth capability out of the documented model override path and makes the capability mapping inconsistent.

Step 1 now prefers `spec.json`, but still instructs the agent to read a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section from the selected artifact. `spec.json` stores this data as `related_conventions[]`, so the preferred path has no such section and can cause the type-design flow to miss required convention inputs unless it falls back to the rendered view despite declaring JSON authoritative. Specify `related_conventions[]` handling for `spec.json`, and use the Markdown heading only for `spec.md` / `plan.md`.

The design command still requires presenting the type design to the user for review before writing artifacts. That conflicts with the newly added ceremony-minimization policy and `/track:plan` gate policy, which say artifact generation should use post-hoc review and pre-approval is reserved for irreversible actions. Keeping this pre-write acceptance loop preserves the old approval ceremony for a reversible generated artifact.

`/track:design` now prefers `spec.json`, but this step still tells the agent to read conventions from a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section. `spec.json` has a `related_conventions[]` field rather than Markdown headings, so the preferred path can fail to load required conventions whenever `spec.json` exists. The instruction should branch on artifact type and read `related_conventions[]` from JSON.

The default capability assignment omits `adr-editor` even though the new profile defines it as a Claude capability and the same rule file later includes it in provider resolution. This leaves the top-level routing summary inconsistent with the registry and the new back-and-forth ADR flow.

`/track:design` now prefers `spec.json`, but this step still instructs the command to read a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section from the selected artifact. Existing `spec.json` stores this as the `related_conventions` field, so when the preferred JSON SSoT exists the command cannot follow its own convention-loading instruction and may skip required conventions.

The staged diff adds references to `adr-editor.md`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is untracked and absent from `git diff --cached`. If the current patch is committed as staged, these references and the new `adr-editor` capability point at a missing subagent definition.

The user workflow still describes `/track:plan` as doing approval confirmation and creating track artifacts only after user approval, which contradicts the new ceremony-minimization convention and the rewritten `/track:plan` post-hoc artifact review flow. This leaves the public guide telling users to follow the removed approval ceremony.

This convention says `spec.json / 型カタログ / impl-plan` should all cite ADRs directly via `AdrRef`, but the new SoT Chain in `/track:plan` only allows the spec layer to reference ADRs and explicitly forbids `type catalogue → ADR` layer skips. If agents follow this, type catalogues or implementation plans can be authored with forbidden direct ADR references instead of routing through spec/type refs.

`/track:design` still instructs design-phase artifacts to cite ADRs via `AdrRef`. That conflicts with the Phase 2 contract described in `/track:plan`, where type catalogue entries are evaluated through `spec_refs[]` and direct ADR references are a layer skip.

The type-designer output contract allows catalogue `informal_grounds[]` to be promoted to `AdrRef`, which again permits type catalogue entries to bypass the spec layer. Under the new SoT Chain this should be limited to `SpecRef` / appropriate convention refs, with ADR rationale propagated through `spec_refs[]`.

This convention says `spec.json`, type catalogues, and `impl-plan` all cite ADRs directly through `AdrRef`. That conflicts with the SoT Chain in `/track:plan`, where Phase 2 catalogue entries reference the spec via `spec_refs[]` and `type catalogue → ADR` is explicitly a forbidden layer skip. Agents following this convention can author downstream artifacts with direct ADR refs that the Phase 2 contract does not allow.

`/track:design` repeats the forbidden direct ADR path by telling design-phase artifacts to cite an ADR via `SpecRef` / `AdrRef`. The rest of the new plan says the type-contract gate evaluates `spec_refs[]` only and forbids `type catalogue → ADR`, so this command can instruct the type-designer/orchestrator to produce invalid SoT-chain references.

The type-designer output contract allows catalogue `informal_grounds[]` to be promoted to `AdrRef`, which lets type catalogue entries bypass the spec layer. Under the new Phase 2 contract, catalogue entries should resolve informal grounds to `SpecRef` or leave them informal until the spec/ADR is updated upstream; otherwise the agent can emit references that `/track:plan` defines as layer skips.

The standard flow still starts users at `/track:plan <feature>` and describes the old pre-artifact approval flow, but the new strict workflow requires a pre-track ADR before `/track:plan` and uses post-hoc artifact review. Following this section skips the required ADR authoring/selection step and can hit the new `/track:plan` pre-flight stop.

`adr-editor` is added to the documented capability table, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is untracked and absent from `git diff HEAD` (`git status` shows `??`). If this patch is committed as-is, the new `subagent_type: "adr-editor"` references a missing agent definition.

The default capability assignment omits `adr-editor`, even though the same rule later routes `adr-editor` to Claude Code and the profile config defines it. This leaves the agent-facing routing guidance internally inconsistent for the new auto-invoked ADR editor capability.

The inserted `## 1.2 Track 前段階 + 3 フェーズ構成` section did not renumber the existing `## 1.2 Project Conventions` section. Later references such as `§1.2` are now ambiguous, so users can be sent to the wrong workflow section.

`/track:plan` forbids the main orchestrator from directly editing `spec.json` or any type catalogue during back-and-forth and says to re-invoke the original writer, but the new writer agents are explicitly read-only/advisory and state that the orchestrator performs the actual writes (`spec-designer` lines 28 and 89-90, `type-designer` lines 120-123). That leaves no actor authorized to persist Phase 1/2 fixes after a red signal, making the documented loop infeasible. Align the ownership model so either the orchestrator is allowed to apply writer output, or the specialist agents are actual file writers.

The quickstart command table still documents the old flow: `/track:plan` is described as planning only with domain type declaration left to `/track:design`, and as creating track artifacts after approval. The note at line 112 also shows `/track:plan -> approval -> /track:design -> /track:implement`, while the updated workflow later in the same file says `/track:plan` runs ADR pre-check plus Phase 0-3 including `/track:design`, and uses post-hoc artifact review rather than pre-approval. This user-facing guide now gives contradictory instructions for the primary planning path.

The default profile declares an `adr-editor` Claude capability and the docs route to `subagent_type: "adr-editor"`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is not tracked by git (`git ls-files` omits it; it is only untracked in this worktree). A committed PR from this diff would leave the runnable profile pointing at a missing custom agent definition.

This says the main orchestrator re-invokes the writer and then applies the proposal to `knowledge/adr/`, `spec.json`, or catalogues. That contradicts the new ADR convention and `adr-editor` contract, which require `adr-editor` itself to edit ADR files and explicitly forbid main from directly editing `knowledge/adr/*.md`; following this text would violate the writer boundary.

The example says a Phase 2 design red signal directly invokes `adr-editor`, while `/track:plan`'s back-and-forth table routes Phase 2 red to `spec-designer` and only escalates to ADR editing if the mismatch propagates upstream. The two instructions give conflicting writer choices for the same failure mode.

`/track:design` still instructs agents to read `metadata.json` for task definitions, but the refactor makes Phase 0 metadata identity-only and moves tasks to Phase 3 `impl-plan.json`, which does not exist yet when Phase 2 design runs. This leaves the command asking for unavailable/stale data.

The orchestration rule's source-of-truth section still models a track around `metadata.json` plus rendered `spec.md`/`plan.md`, omitting the new authoritative artifacts (`spec.json`, `<layer>-types.json`, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json`). Agents following this mandatory read list can skip the JSON SSoTs introduced by the refactor.

`/track:design` still requires a pre-write user review loop for generated type catalogues: it says to present the design before writing and iterate until the user accepts it. This contradicts the new ceremony-minimization policy and `/track:plan` gate policy, which restrict pre-approval to irreversible actions and require artifact generation to use post-hoc review of the actual files. Because `/track:plan` routes Phase 2 through `/track:design`, following this command preserves the obsolete pre-approval ceremony for a reversible track artifact.

The orchestration rule now defines `spec.json`, `<layer>-types.json`, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json` as the Phase 1-3 SSoTs, but the mandatory “Read these first” source-of-truth list still only includes `metadata.json`, rendered `spec.md`/`plan.md`, and `verification.md`. Agents following this rule before planning or implementation can skip the new authoritative JSON artifacts and work from rendered views, undermining the refactor’s SSoT chain.

The default capability list omits `adr-editor`, even though this same refactor adds `capabilities.adr-editor` to `.harness/config/agent-profiles.json` and later sections describe `adr-editor` as a default Claude Code capability. This leaves the top-level user guide internally inconsistent about the available/default specialist capabilities for the new back-and-forth ADR workflow.

DEVELOPER_AI_WORKFLOW.md still describes `/track:design` as declaring only `domain-types.json`/domain types, while the refactored command and new Phase 2 flow now process every TDDD-enabled layer and produce `<layer>-types.json` catalogues. The same domain-only wording is repeated later in the standard flow, so the user-facing workflow remains inconsistent with the new multi-layer type-designer contract.

The Bash permission prompt still tells reviewers to replace `bin/sotp spec approve` with `cargo make spec-approve`, but this wrapper has been removed. That leaves the permission flow pointing at a non-existent command and will mis-handle direct `bin/sotp spec approve` requests.

The PermissionRequest prompt still treats `bin/sotp spec approve` as a valid direct command and maps it to `cargo make spec-approve`, even though this wrapper is being removed. That leaves a stale permission path that will keep suggesting a nonexistent alternative for the deleted command.

The `PermissionRequest` prompt still tells users to replace `bin/sotp spec approve` with `cargo make spec-approve`, but that wrapper was removed from the allowlist and is no longer part of the harness policy. Hitting this hook will now produce a dead-end suggestion for a nonexistent command.

`adr-editor.md` is added to `REQUIRED_AGENT_FILES`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is not tracked in the diff/index (it only appears as an untracked working-tree file). On a clean checkout of the committed changes, `verify_agent_definitions` will require this file and fail `verify-orchestra-local` unless the agent file is added to the tracked changes or removed from the required list.

The new `spec.md` frontmatter no longer matches the legacy markdown validators that are still reachable through the CLI and these modules' fallback paths. `verify_spec_schema` handles `spec.json`, but `spec_frontmatter`, `spec_signals`, and `spec_attribution` still expect the old markdown format (`status` plus `[source: ...]` tags) when no sibling `spec.json` exists, so a rendered spec.md from this change will now fail direct verification.

Placeholder scanning now drops the `file` half of every structured reference. `adr_refs`, `convention_refs`, and top-level `related_conventions` only contribute anchors to `all_texts`, so a placeholder in a path like `knowledge/adr/{{date}}.md` will no longer be detected even though the comment says the pass scans all text-bearing strings.

Schema-v2 decode defaults `goal`/`constraints`/`acceptance_criteria` to empty, but `encode()` always writes them back out because these fields are missing `skip_serializing_if`. That makes `encode(decode(json))` add empty arrays to minimal spec files and breaks the round-trip/idempotence expected by the migration.

The attribution verifier no longer checks `goal` items at all. Since `goal` is now a `Vec<SpecRequirement>`, a goal requirement with no typed refs will pass this gate even though the same requirement would be flagged in scope/constraints/AC.

The legacy YAML fallback still requires `status`, but schema v2 `spec.md` no longer emits that field. If `verify()` runs on a `spec.md` without a sibling `spec.json`, a valid v2 frontmatter will be rejected as missing `status`.

DTO deserialization still accepts unknown fields, so schema-v2 `spec.json` files that keep removed v1 fields (`status`, `approved_at`, `content_hash`, `sources`, `task_refs`) will decode successfully instead of failing validation. `verify_spec_schema()` therefore does not actually enforce the new schema unless unknown-field rejection or explicit checks are added.

The legacy markdown fallback still requires `status`, but `render_spec()` no longer emits it. Any rendered `spec.md` that is verified without a sibling `spec.json` will now fail the fallback path even though it was produced by this code.

The markdown fallbacks in `spec_signals.rs` and `spec_attribution.rs` still parse legacy `### S-/REQ-` headings and `[source: ...]` tags. `render_spec()` now emits bullet requirements with `adr_refs`/`convention_refs`/`informal_grounds`, so a v2 `spec.md` without sibling `spec.json` will be misvalidated: `spec_signals` rejects it as all-red, while `spec_attribution` silently sees no requirements at all.

Placeholder scanning in `validate_spec_json_file()` never inspects `SpecRequirement.id()`. Because `SpecElementId` still accepts valid-looking placeholders like `TBD-01`, the latest-track gate can miss stub IDs on goal/scope/constraint/AC items.

Several nested DTOs here still deserialize permissively (`SpecScopeDto`, `SignalCountsDto`, `AdrRefDto`, `ConventionRefDto`, `InformalGroundRefDto`), so stray keys inside `scope`, `signals`, or typed refs are silently dropped instead of being rejected by the v2 schema. That breaks the validation contract and the encode -> decode -> encode round-trip guarantee. The same `SignalCountsDto` omission exists in `libs/infrastructure/src/verify/spec_frontmatter.rs`.

This `pass` shortcut trusts a spoofable comment string instead of validating the v2 markdown body. Any hand-authored or stale `spec.md` can evade signal verification by prepending the generated header, and the same pattern in `spec_attribution.rs` bypasses attribution checks as well. If `spec.json` is absent, this should fail closed or still parse the document rather than returning success.

The generated-v2 guard only checks `content.starts_with("<!-- Generated from spec.json")`, so a `spec.md` with a UTF-8 BOM or even a leading blank line bypasses the fail-closed path and falls back to the legacy heading scan. That lets a generated v2 `spec.md` without a sibling `spec.json` pass attribution verification instead of being rejected.

Serializing `AdrRef`/`ConventionRef` with `to_string_lossy()` makes `spec.json` silently corrupt any non-UTF-8 `PathBuf`, so encode->decode is no longer lossless and a document can come back pointing at a different file. Either reject non-UTF-8 paths or serialize them losslessly.

The new v2 guard only trips when the generated header is present. A migrated `spec.md` that has the new bullet/typed-ref layout but is missing that header still falls through to the old `### S-/REQ-` scanner, which ignores the new requirement lines entirely, so an unattributed v2 spec can pass when `spec.json` is absent.

The new fail-closed branch only triggers when a parsed frontmatter block is present and it does not contain `status:`. A stale or hand-edited v2 `spec.md` that still has `status:` and no `### S-`/`### REQ-` headings will return `pass` here instead of forcing a `spec.json`-backed attribution check, so the attribution gate can be bypassed.

`spec_frontmatter` still accepts legacy lifecycle frontmatter: `status` is explicitly allowed and any other unknown key is flattened/ignored, so a `spec.md` containing removed v2 fields such as `status`, `approved_at`, or `content_hash` can pass this verifier whenever there is no sibling `spec.json`. The briefing requires the frontmatter/schema check to enforce the new schema with the approved-lifecycle fields removed.

The fallback frontmatter verifier still accepts the removed v1 lifecycle fields: `status` is explicitly allowed and all other unknown fields are flattened/ignored, so a `spec.md` with `approved_at` or `content_hash` passes whenever no sibling `spec.json` exists. That violates the v2 validation requirement that removed approved-lifecycle fields be rejected rather than silently accepted.

`sync_rendered_views` still treats every `SpecCodecError::Json` as a recoverable malformed-JSON warning. With the new `deny_unknown_fields` DTOs, a well-formed legacy/v1 `spec.json` containing removed fields like `status` now surfaces as this Json error, so the renderer silently skips it instead of failing the unsupported schema. This masks the no-longer-supported v1 path and can leave stale or missing `spec.md` output.

The markdown fallback path still accepts removed schema-v1 frontmatter fields when no sibling spec.json exists. SpecFrontmatterDto keeps status optional and flattens all unknown fields into _extra, so frontmatter containing approved_at or content_hash deserializes successfully and verify() returns pass. The briefing requires spec_frontmatter to validate the new schema and reject the removed approved fields; this leaves direct spec.md verification unable to catch stale v1 lifecycle metadata.

`ImplPlanDocumentDto` only applies `deny_unknown_fields` at the top level. Because it reuses `PlanDocument`, `PlanSectionDocument`, and `TrackTaskDocument` from `track::codec`, unknown fields nested under `plan`, `sections`, or `tasks` are still accepted and silently dropped, so this codec does not fully enforce the strict schema boundary described in the briefing.

`TaskCoverageDocumentDto` deserializes each section into a `BTreeMap`, so repeated JSON object keys are silently overwritten by serde instead of being rejected. A malformed `task-coverage.json` can therefore drop earlier refs from the same section and change coverage semantics without failing validation.

`parse_task_status()` accepts a `commit_hash` on any task status but silently drops it unless `status == "done"`. A malformed record like `{"status":"todo","commit_hash":"abc1234"}` is therefore accepted and re-encoded without the hash, which is silent data loss instead of a validation failure. Reject non-`done` tasks that carry a non-null `commit_hash`.

`validate_plan_file` now skips the task-item check whenever the rendered text merely contains `impl-plan.json`. That is a content-based bypass: any hand-edited `plan.md` that mentions the filename will pass even if it has no task list, so the latest-track gate no longer enforces the rule it claims to.

`codec::encode()` now always emits `schema_version = 4`, but this round-trip test still asserts that the version survives encode/decode unchanged. The archived fixture at `track/archive/filelock-migration-2026-03-11/metadata.json` is schema_version 2, so this test will fail in CI until the assertion is updated to expect the version bump.

`text.contains("impl-plan.json")` matches every new `plan.md` because the generated header already contains that substring. `validate_plan_file` therefore stops enforcing that the latest track has any task items.

`load_impl_plan_task_ids` collapses read/decode failures into an empty set, and the `!valid_task_ids.is_empty()` guard then skips referential validation entirely. A present-but-empty or malformed `impl-plan.json` can therefore let arbitrary `task_ref`s pass.

A `metadata.json` that parses but omits or garbles `schema_version` is treated as `0` and skipped as a legacy track. That hides malformed v4 metadata from the freshness verifier instead of reporting it.

`validate_plan_file` skips the task-item requirement whenever the rendered text merely contains `impl-plan.json`. That is a content-based bypass: a hand-edited `plan.md` can include that substring and still pass even if it has no task list.

`load_impl_plan_task_ids` collapses read/decode failures into `None`, and the caller turns that into an empty set. A present-but-malformed `impl-plan.json` therefore disables referential validation entirely and can let bad `task_ref`s pass.

`load_impl_plan_task_ids` treats any read/decode failure in `impl-plan.json` as `None`, so `spec_coverage::verify` silently skips referential-integrity checks when the companion file exists but is malformed. That can let bad `task_ref`s pass CI instead of failing closed.

The legacy-track skip is keyed off `schema_version < 4`, but a metadata file with a missing or non-numeric `schema_version` is converted to `0` and skipped as if it were legacy. That means a corrupted v4 `metadata.json` can bypass freshness verification entirely instead of surfacing an error.

`impl-plan.json` referential checks are skipped whenever `valid_task_ids` is empty, so a present-but-empty implementation plan is treated the same as “no plan” and `task-coverage.json` task_refs are never validated. That lets coverage reference non-existent tasks without failing CI.

The latest-track plan validator uses `text.contains("impl-plan.json")` to detect the transitional stub. Any plan.md that merely mentions that filename now bypasses the task-item check, so a malformed latest plan with no checklist can pass verification.

`render_registry` still special-cases only `schema_version == 3` branchless planned tracks. After this migration, new plan-only tracks are written as schema_version 4, and `domain::track_phase::resolve_phase` already treats v4 branchless planned tracks as activatable. As a result, a newer plan-only track can incorrectly win the "Latest active track" slot and drive the wrong next command in `registry.md`.

The new stub detection skips the task-items check whenever `plan.md` merely contains the stub sentence. That sentence is not unique to machine-generated output, so a handwritten plan can include it and bypass validation even if it has no task items. This weakens the latest-track gate into a string-match that is easy to spoof.

`decode()` strips `tasks`/`plan` before deserializing, and `TrackDocumentV2` does not reject unknown fields. As a result, a schema_version 4 `metadata.json` that still contains legacy `tasks`/`plan` is accepted instead of being rejected, so the new identity-only schema is not actually enforced for v4 files.

Referential-integrity checks only cover `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria`. Any `task_ref` placed under `constraints` or `out_of_scope` is never compared against `impl-plan.json`, so invalid references in those sections slip through even when an impl-plan is present.

`spec.md` is still rendered from `spec.json` alone. The new sibling `task-coverage.json` is never loaded here, so coverage edits cannot affect the generated spec view and freshness checks stay blind to the new coverage document.

`decode()` strips `tasks`/`plan` before it checks `schema_version`, so a malformed v4 `metadata.json` that still carries legacy fields is silently accepted and those fields are discarded instead of being rejected. That weakens the identity-only contract and can hide corrupt metadata.

The stub exemption for latest-track validation is based only on the two marker strings. It never checks whether sibling `impl-plan.json` is actually absent, so a hand-edited `plan.md` that copies the stub header/note will bypass the task-item check even when a real implementation plan exists.

`sync_rendered_views(root, Some(id))` still calls `collect_track_snapshots(root)` before it narrows to the requested track, so a malformed sibling `metadata.json` aborts a targeted sync and the single-track path is no longer isolated from unrelated tracks.

`selection_priority` still treats `schema_version != 3` as a high-priority branchless active track. With the new v4 planning-only schema, a branchless planned track now gets priority 2 instead of being deprioritized, so `latest_track` can pick a planning-only track as the latest active track ahead of a real active branch track.

`selection_priority` still treats only `schema_version == 3` as the planning-only case. With the new v4 identity-only tracks, a branchless planned v4 track is scored as priority 2, so `latest_track_dir` can pick it over a newer branchful active track and validate the wrong track. The v4 deprioritization added in `render_registry` needs to be mirrored here.

`decode()` strips `tasks` and `plan` before it ever inspects `schema_version`, so a malformed v4 `metadata.json` that still carries legacy fields is silently accepted and normalized instead of being rejected. That lets stale identity-only files slip through validation.

The new referential-integrity pass only checks `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria`, but the task-coverage schema also carries `out_of_scope` and `constraints`. Invalid task IDs in those sections are never validated, even though the surrounding comment says all `task_refs` should be checked against `impl-plan.json`.

The stub note points users to `/track:impl-plan`, but the repo's actual workflow command is `/track:implement` (see `track_phase::NextCommand::Implement`). This generated fallback currently directs users to a non-existent command.

`impl-plan.json` and `metadata.json` are now persisted through separate writes with no shared transaction or lock. A crash or I/O failure after `save_impl_plan` but before the metadata update leaves task state and track status out of sync, so readers can observe inconsistent SSoT files.

This still rejects a legitimate `impl-plan.json` with zero tasks: `render_plan(Some(doc))` emits no task items for an empty document, but this check only exempts the stub when `impl-plan.json` is absent. Because empty impl-plans are valid elsewhere in the codebase, latest-track verification will now fail on an otherwise valid track.

`validate_plan_file` now skips the task-item check whenever `impl-plan.json` exists and decodes to zero tasks. That lets a stale transition stub (`metadata.json + impl-plan.json` header plus the `impl-plan.json not yet generated` note) pass validation after an empty impl-plan is committed, even though the renderer should emit the generated empty-plan layout instead of the stub.

This rule now returns priority 0 for branchless v4 tracks unless they are `planned`, so a valid v4 track in `in_progress`/`blocked`/`cancelled` can be ranked below an older branchless planned track. `latest_track_dir()` will then select the wrong track.

Referential integrity for `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria` task refs is only checked while iterating requirements that still exist in `spec.json`. Extra or stale keys in those two `task-coverage.json` sections are never visited, so a file can contain `in_scope: {"ZZ-01": ["T999"]}` with an `impl-plan.json` that lacks `T999` and still pass as long as `ZZ-01` is not in the spec. The new contract says task refs in all four coverage sections must be validated against `impl-plan.json`; iterate the coverage maps for `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria` as well, not only the spec requirements.

`resolve_path` accepts absolute paths verbatim and also joins relative paths without any containment check. Because ref fields come from repo content, a crafted `file` value can point the verifier at files outside the repository and probe host file existence / contents instead of staying under the track root.

The canonical JSON encoder double-quotes object keys: `escape_json_string()` already returns a JSON string literal, and the caller wraps it in another pair of quotes. That produces malformed canonical JSON like `""id"":...`, so hashes computed here will not match hashes produced by any correct canonicalizer and valid `SpecRef`s will be rejected.

Referenced `spec.json` files are only parsed as raw `serde_json::Value` here, so the verifier never runs `spec_codec::decode` on the target file. A syntactically valid but schema-invalid spec (for example one with duplicate requirement ids or malformed required fields) can still satisfy `SpecRef` anchor/hash checks and pass `plan-artifact-refs`, which defeats the intended spec-validation step.

`resolve_path` loses the repository-root constraint when `verify` is called with a relative track directory, which is exactly what the CLI help accepts (`--track-dir track/items/<id>`). For a relative `track_dir`, walking three parents can produce the empty path; `canonicalize()` then falls back to that empty path, and `resolved_canonical.starts_with(&root_canonical)` no longer prevents `../` escapes. That means a ref can point outside the repository and still pass file-existence or SpecRef validation if the outside target exists, violating the verifier's path-traversal guard.

`resolve_path` does not reliably enforce the repo-root containment check when `track_dir` is relative, which is the normal shape for `bin/sotp plan-artifact-refs --track-dir track/items/<id>`. For `track/items/<id>`, the three-parent walk yields an empty `repo_root`; when canonicalization falls back to that empty path, `starts_with` no longer constrains the resolved target to the repository. A ref such as `../outside.md` or an outside `spec.json` can therefore pass file existence/hash validation instead of being rejected as path traversal. Canonicalize or absolutize `track_dir`/repo root before joining refs, and reject any path whose canonical target is outside that root.

`resolve_path` only rejects traversal when `resolved.canonicalize()` succeeds. If a ref contains `..` and the target does not exist, the fallback `resolved.clone()` is checked with `starts_with(repo_root)`, which is lexical and does not collapse parent components, so a traversal path can slip through as a plain "file not found" instead of being rejected as invalid. Normalize the path components before the containment check.

`spec.json` and referenced catalogue/spec files are read with `read_to_string` without the shared `symlink_guard` that the rest of `infrastructure::verify` uses. A symlinked `spec.json` or `spec_ref.file` can therefore redirect verification to an arbitrary host file outside the repo, bypassing the intended containment check.

Deriving `trusted_root` from `track_dir.canonicalize()` follows symlinked track directories/ancestors, which shifts the verifier's trust boundary to the symlink target. Because `reject_symlinks_below` treats `trusted_root` as implicitly safe, a symlinked `track/items/<id>` can make this check accept and read spec/catalogue files outside the repository. Use the non-following trusted-root resolver or reject symlinked `track_dir` before building paths.

Treating `!spec_json_path.is_file()` as “spec.json absent” lets a symlinked or otherwise non-regular `spec.json` bypass the verifier entirely. `reject_symlinks_below()` never runs for that path, so a track can skip all plan-artifact checks by replacing `spec.json` with a symlink to a directory or a broken link.

`resolve_path()` collapses `..` components before symlink inspection, so a ref like `dir/symlink/../target.md` can normalize to `dir/target.md` and evade `reject_symlinks_below()`. If `symlink` points outside the repo, the containment check is no longer sound for that path.

`trusted_root` is derived by walking three parents up from `track_dir` instead of using a vetted repo root. If the workspace sits under a symlinked prefix, `reject_symlinks_below` stops at this derived root and never inspects ancestors above it, so a ref can escape the real repository boundary. Use the shared trusted-root resolver or require the caller to pass an already-validated repo root.

`resolve_path()` treats an empty or dot-only `file` as valid by joining it to `repo_root`, so malformed file-based refs like `""` or `"."` are accepted as if they pointed at the repository root. That lets bad `AdrRef`/`ConventionRef` entries pass verification instead of being rejected as invalid.

Root selection is split between `resolve_trusted_root()` and the hard-coded three-parent walk in `resolve_path()`. When `--track-dir` is provided from a different cwd, `resolve_trusted_root()` can fall back to `spec_path.parent()` while `resolve_path()` still resolves refs against the assumed repo root. That means the symlink guard and the path containment check can run against different trees, so refs may be validated outside the intended repository root or rejected incorrectly.

The referential-integrity check uses one global spec-id set, so a key in any task-coverage section is accepted if that id exists anywhere in spec.json. Since task-coverage.json is defined as four section-specific maps, entries like `constraints["GO-01"]` or `out_of_scope["CO-01"]` can pass even though they do not resolve to an element in the corresponding spec section. This lets malformed coverage mappings pass the new gate for optional sections; validate each task-coverage map against the matching spec section's id set instead of a global set.

Inline example markers on the opening fence are ignored. The scanner only checks the preceding line and the block contents, so a long block tagged inline on the fence line, such as ```example or ```json example, still emits a canonical-block warning even though the briefing requires inline example-marked blocks to be excluded. Include the opening fence/info string in the marker check and cover it with a unit test.

The canonical-block suppression only checks the line before the fence and lines inside the block. The T011 brief also requires inline `example` markers to suppress warnings, but an opening fence like ```json example followed by >10 lines will still emit a canonical-block suspicion because the opening line is never tested for the marker. This creates false warnings for explicitly example-tagged blocks; include the opening fence/info string in the marker check and add a regression test for inline markers.

The canonical-block scanner suppresses a warning when any line inside the fenced body matches an example marker. That means ordinary long blocks containing text like `example:` or `// example` will be skipped, even though the brief only calls for deliberate preceding-comment or inline tags. This creates false negatives for the new warning.

Fence closing is detected only by fence character, not by the opening fence length. A block opened with 4+ backticks or tildes can therefore be treated as closed by a shorter 3-char fence, which truncates the counted block and can miss >10-line suspicions on valid Markdown.

When `task-coverage.json` is absent, this branch only emits a warning and never inspects `impl-plan.json`, so a present-but-malformed impl plan can be silently skipped. The brief says malformed `impl-plan.json` must fail closed whenever the file is present.

The canonical-block scanner trims every line before fence detection, so a line like `    ```rust` is treated as a fenced block even though CommonMark parses it as indented code. That can produce warning findings for content that is not actually a fenced code block.

Example suppression only checks the first line inside the fence. If a deliberate example tag appears after a blank first line, or later in the block, the verifier still emits a canonical-block warning even though example-tagged blocks are supposed to be excluded.

The canonical-block suppression is too broad: any interior line that happens to match `is_example_marker()` disables the warning, so an ordinary long fenced block that contains literal `// example` or `example:` text will be silently skipped. The brief only calls for deliberate example tags, not arbitrary matches anywhere in the body.

`inner` is fully trimmed before fence-close detection, so a line like `    ``` ` inside a fenced block is treated as a real closing fence even though CommonMark only allows up to 3 leading spaces. That can terminate the scan early and undercount a long block, suppressing the canonical-block warning.

The canonical-block suppression logic is wired to `example` markers (`<!-- example:`, `// example`, `# example`, `example:` and fence info strings containing `example`), but the ADR/Q3 convention is `<!-- illustrative, non-canonical -->`. Legitimate example blocks will still emit warnings, and the new tests only cover the wrong marker form, so this gap is untested.

Treating `plan.md`/`verification.md` symlink-guard failures as warnings fails open: a symlinked or otherwise unsafe doc path skips canonical-block scanning entirely without an error finding, unlike the rest of this verifier.

The canonical-block scanner skips any fence line with 4+ leading spaces, which misses valid fenced code blocks nested inside Markdown containers such as list items. Long fenced blocks in those contexts will not trigger the >10-line warning.

The task-coverage SpecElementId integrity check uses one global spec-id set for every section, including goal IDs. That lets malformed section mappings pass, for example `constraints["IN-01"]` or `in_scope["GO-01"]`, as long as the ID exists somewhere in `spec.json`. Since `task-coverage.json` is defined as four section-specific maps, validate each map against the matching spec section and exclude `goal` IDs.

When `task-coverage.json` is absent, the verifier only emits a warning and never inspects a present `impl-plan.json`. The brief requires malformed `impl-plan.json` to fail closed when present, so a track with a malformed impl plan and missing coverage currently returns an OK outcome with only the soft missing-coverage warning.

When `task-coverage.json` is absent, `verify()` returns the warning at this branch and never inspects a present `impl-plan.json`. That lets a malformed impl plan slip through this verifier instead of failing closed, which contradicts the T011 requirement that malformed `impl-plan.json` must be reported when present.

The canonical-block scanner only looks at lines after stripping up to three leading spaces, so fenced blocks nested under Markdown container markers such as blockquotes are never recognized. A long fenced block in those contexts will not emit the required suspicion warning, creating a bypass for the new check.

The new canonical-block scanner uses direct string slicing in non-test code (`raw_line[leading_spaces..]` here, and the same pattern again for `inner_raw[inner_leading..]` later). This crate inherits the workspace lint `clippy::indexing_slicing = "deny"`, so `cargo make clippy` will fail even though the slice bounds are intended to be safe. Use a non-indexing path such as `strip_prefix`/iterator-based trimming before this can pass CI.

Treating `std::fs::read_to_string` failures as warnings lets an unreadable `plan.md` / `verification.md` bypass the canonical-block scan entirely. Since symlink guarding already distinguishes absence from presence, a present-file read failure should be an error, not a warning.

Fence detection only strips leading ASCII spaces before looking for ` ``` ` / `~~~` at column 0, so fenced blocks nested inside blockquotes or list items are invisible to this scan. That lets long canonical blocks evade the warning in common Markdown layouts.

The fence-body scan strips blockquote prefixes before checking for a closing fence. Inside an open fenced block, `>` is literal content, so a line like `> ```rust` can be mistaken for the terminator and suppress a long-block warning.

Example suppression only checks the raw preceding line. A quoted marker such as `> <!-- illustrative, non-canonical -->` before a quoted fence will still warn, even though the scanner claims to handle blockquote-nested blocks.

The canonical-block scanner will miss fenced blocks that are nested under list items or other Markdown containers with 4+ leading spaces. It only strips blockquote prefixes, then skips any line with four or more spaces before checking for ```/~~~, so a long fenced block indented by a list marker is never warned on.

`normalize_for_fence()` trims all leading whitespace after `strip_container_prefix()`, so a 4-space-indented literal block like `    ```rust` is treated as a fenced block. That makes the new canonical-block scanner misclassify indented code samples and can also suppress the intended `example` exclusion behavior inside blockquotes/list items because the preceding-line check never normalizes container prefixes.

Example-marker suppression is only applied to the raw previous line. Because fence detection already normalizes blockquote/list prefixes, a fenced block inside one of those containers that is tagged with `<!-- illustrative, non-canonical -->` will still emit a warning, so the documented exclusion does not work for nested Markdown containers.

The canonical-block scanner normalizes every inner line before checking for the closing fence. That strips blockquote/list prefixes from code content, so a literal line inside a fenced block like `> ```rust` or `- ```rust` can be misread as the closing fence. The scan then terminates early and can miss the warning for a genuinely long block.

The canonical-block suppression heuristic is too broad and too narrow at the same time: `# example` / `example:` / `contains("example")` will suppress warnings for ordinary Markdown headings or unrelated labels, while the common `<!-- example -->` marker is not recognized. That makes the new warning check unreliable because non-example blocks can be skipped and tagged example blocks can still warn.

Closing-fence detection strips blockquote prefixes inside the fenced body, so a literal line like `> ``` ` is treated as a closer. That can terminate the scan early and suppress the >10-line warning for long blocks that contain quoted fence text.

Opening-fence normalization is not re-applied after stripping a list-item marker. A fence nested in a list item with a blockquote marker on the same line (for example `- > ```rust`) is left as `> ```rust` and never recognized, so some long fenced blocks are invisible to the suspicion scan.

`normalize_for_closing_fence()` only calls `trim()`, so closing-fence detection does not apply the same CommonMark normalization as opening-fence detection. A fenced block inside a blockquote can fail to close until EOF, while an indented line like `    ````, which should be treated as content, can be mistaken for a closing fence. This will produce spurious or missed canonical-block warnings.

The canonical-block suppression is too broad: `fence_info_has_example()` treats any fence info string containing `example` as exempt, so fences like ` ```notexample ` or ` ```exampleish ` suppress the warning even though they are not example markers. The preceding-line path has a similar false-negative risk because it falls back to trimmed raw text for non-fence lines, so literal code lines containing `example` can also suppress detection. That lets non-example blocks evade the new warning gate.

`scan_canonical_block_suspicion()` can stop early on literal content inside a fenced block: `normalize_for_closing_fence()` strips blockquote prefixes from every inner line, so a body line like `> ```...` or `> ~~~...` is mistaken for a real closing fence. That can truncate the scan and suppress the long-block warning the brief requires.

`is_example_marker()` is overbroad for Markdown docs: it suppresses warnings for plain prose/heading lines such as `# example` and `example:` even when they are not explicit example tags. In `plan.md` / `verification.md`, that can hide a canonical block accidentally.

The canonical-block scanner derives `stripped_prefix` from `line.len() - content.len()`, but `content` has already had trailing whitespace trimmed. On a valid opener like `> ```rust   `, the stored prefix includes part of the fence, so `close_fence_normalize()` never matches the real closer and the rest of the file gets treated as one block. That creates spurious warnings and can suppress later scans.

This module now spans roughly 2k lines, far beyond the repo's 700-line module cap, while also mixing verifier logic, the canonical-block heuristic, and a large regression suite in one file. That is an architecture violation and makes the change materially harder to review and maintain.

Canonical-block detection is whitespace-unsafe: `open_fence_normalize()` derives `stripped_prefix` from `line.len() - content.len()`, but `content` has already had trailing whitespace trimmed, so an opening fence like ```rust   can capture part of the fence payload as the prefix. Combined with `close_fence_normalize()` using `trim()`, this can both fail to find the real closing fence and accept an indented body line as a closer, making the new warning check unreliable for valid Markdown.

`close_fence_normalize()` requires the closing fence to start with the exact raw prefix stripped from the opener. That is stricter than CommonMark and breaks valid fenced blocks inside list items or blockquotes where the closing fence can use a different allowed indentation/container prefix (for example, a list-item opener like `- ```rust` closes with an indented fence line, not `- ```rust`). In those cases the scanner walks to EOF, miscomputes the block length, and skips the rest of the document, so later canonical-block suspicions are never checked.

Canonical-block normalization trims all leading whitespace after `strip_container_prefix`, so the intended 4+ space indented-code-block guard is defeated. A line like `    ```rust` will still be treated as a fenced block, which can emit spurious warnings for literal markdown examples and makes the scanner misclassify common rendered-doc patterns.

When `task-coverage.json` is absent this branch still symlink-checks and decodes `impl-plan.json`, turning a malformed/partial impl plan into an error. The T011 behavior for absent task coverage is warning-only soft failure, and impl-plan task-id integrity is only needed to validate task refs from an existing `task-coverage.json`; this can make `bin/sotp verify plan-artifact-refs` fail CI for a track that should only receive the missing-coverage warning.

`fence_line_normalize()` strips all leading whitespace before `detect_fence_open()`, so an indented code sample such as `    ```rust` is treated as a fenced block and can emit a canonical-block warning even though CommonMark parses it as an indented code block.

Example suppression does not normalize list-item prefixes, so a tagged block inside a bullet list (for example `- <!-- illustrative, non-canonical -->` or `- <!-- example -->`) still warns. The documented example-marker exclusion therefore fails in nested list contexts.

Canonical-block scanning strips all leading indentation from every body line before fence-close detection. That means an indented literal fence inside a long code sample (for example `    ```rust` or `    ~~~`) is treated as the closing fence, so the block length is undercounted and the >10-line warning can be missed. Preserve indentation for body lines or only accept closing fences with CommonMark's allowed indentation.

Canonical-block scanning strips `>` prefixes when recognizing an opening fence, but the closing-fence search never removes those prefixes. Any fenced block rendered inside a blockquote will therefore be treated as unclosed and can be falsely reported as a >10-line canonical block.

`fence_info_has_example()` only strips a 3-character fence prefix, so valid fenced blocks opened with 4+ backticks or tildes are not recognized as `example`-tagged and will still emit canonical-block warnings. This breaks the stated suppression rule for example blocks.

`fence_line_normalize()` strips all leading whitespace before fence detection, so an indented code sample like `    ```rust` is treated as a fenced block even though CommonMark parses it as an indented code block. The same normalization is applied to body lines, so a literal `> ```rust` can also be mistaken for a closing fence. That makes the canonical-block warning noisy and can miss real long fences.

The fence detector strips all leading whitespace before matching ```/~~~, so a 4-space-indented code sample or other indented literal fence text is treated as a real fenced block and can emit warnings even though the brief only asks to scan fenced blocks. That makes the heuristic fire on valid Markdown and can also terminate a scan early if a literal fence appears inside a real block.

`fence_info_has_example()` is too permissive: it treats any word-boundary occurrence of `example` in the fence info string as a suppression marker, so blocks like ```non-example or ```my-example are excluded even though they are not deliberate example tags. That can hide canonical-block warnings for ordinary fenced blocks.

`usecase::render_track_views` would move Markdown/view rendering and file I/O into the usecase layer, which is the wrong boundary for this repository's hexagonal split. The validation step can be shared, but the render pipeline should stay infrastructure-owned; otherwise this TODO would invert the dependency direction instead of just extracting the stale-signal check.

The new parenthetical still says the workflow's actual Markdown generation and file write happen "via infra port", which is inconsistent with the pure-orchestrator boundary described here and with the existing `contract_map_workflow.rs` pattern. As written, it can mislead implementers into reintroducing the same layer-boundary leak this TODO is trying to remove.

`track/workflow.md` now says the default profile assigns `adr-editor` to Claude Code, but the sample profiles in this change only define `orchestrator`, `spec-designer`, `impl-planner`, `type-designer`, `implementer`, `reviewer`, and `researcher`. That leaves the workflow pointing at a capability users cannot select from the documented profiles, so the ADR back-and-forth path is unusable as written.

`adr-editor` is referenced by the new workflow, but this source-of-truth capability map never defines it. On a red Phase 1 signal the orchestrator will try to re-invoke `adr-editor`, and capability resolution will fail before the ADR edit loop can start. The same omission also exists in the three sample profile files.

The new phased workflow says `/track:plan` orchestrates Phase 0-3 to produce `metadata.json`, `spec.json`, type catalogues, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json`, but the same document still later tells users that `/track:plan` creates the old artifact set (`metadata.json`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `verification.md`) after approval and after clearing `track/tech-stack.md` TODOs.
