The design doc still lists `CC-SDD-02: SpecStatus enum + content hash auto-demotion` as an active key design decision, even though this change removes the spec approval/content-hash gate and the detailed section describing it. Leaving this row makes `knowledge/DESIGN.md` internally inconsistent and preserves the obsolete approval model as current design guidance.

This branch gate now short-circuits unless the checkout is `track/*`, and it downgrades a missing `track/items/$TRACK_ID` directory to `[SKIP]`. That is a behavioral regression: `plan/*` checkouts will never run the verifier, and a malformed `track/*`/`plan/*` branch with no backing track directory will pass instead of failing closed. The task should let the verifier resolve both branch types and surface the missing-directory error.

The public `verify-plan-artifact-refs` task is wired as a host-side `cargo make` wrapper instead of the Docker-compose wrapper called for in the design, so `cargo make verify-plan-artifact-refs` bypasses the sandboxed container path and cache-dir setup used by the other gate tasks.

If the branch matches `track/*` but `track/items/<id>` is missing, the task prints `[SKIP]` and exits successfully. That turns a broken branch-to-track mapping into a pass instead of failing the new integrity check.

`verify-plan-artifact-refs-local` only matches `track/*` branches, so it will never run on `plan/<id>` checkouts even though this Makefile explicitly supports plan branches via `track-plan-branch`. That leaves `cargo make ci` without this gate on planning-only review branches.

If a `track/*` branch is present but `track/items/$TRACK_ID` is missing, the task prints `[SKIP]` and exits successfully. For a CI gate this should fail closed, otherwise a malformed or renamed track branch can bypass plan-artifact verification.

Restricting the local gate to `track/*` branches skips `plan/<id>` checkouts entirely, even though the verifier's active-track resolution already supports `plan/*` as valid context. That means `cargo make ci` will not run `verify-plan-artifact-refs` on planning-only review branches, leaving the new CI gate uncovered there; delegating branch resolution to the CLI would also avoid the manual `track/items/$TRACK_ID` reconstruction.

On a `plan/*` branch, a missing `track/items/<id>` directory is treated as a successful skip. That means `cargo make ci` can pass on a planning branch without running the new branch-bound verifier at all, even though the accepted no-op case is only for branches that are not `track/*` or `plan/*`. This should fail closed like the `track/*` case so broken plan branch to track directory resolution does not silently bypass artifact reference checks.

D1.1 says the ADR must not contain full trait/struct/method specifications, but D2.1 immediately embeds concrete Rust struct definitions for `SpecElementId`, `AdrAnchor`, `ConventionAnchor`, `ContentHash`, and the three ref types. That makes the ADR internally inconsistent about how much type detail it is allowed to spell out.

`SpecElementId` is only validated as a non-empty string in the task/verification text, but the ADR already fixes an ID naming convention (`IN-\d+`, `AC-\d+`, etc.). As written, the implementation can accept arbitrary ids, which weakens `SpecRef.anchor` and the new spec-element identity scheme.

The scope for T001/verification includes `track/workflow.md`, but the ADR rollout section only names `CLAUDE.md` and `DEVELOPER_AI_WORKFLOW.md`. That leaves one of the declared in-scope docs outside the ADR's own migration plan, so the file set is inconsistent across the documents.

D1.5 permits type catalogues to reference conventions, but the concrete ref schema only places `ConventionRef` on spec.json fields and D3.2 later says catalogues have no convention reference field at all. That gives implementers conflicting requirements: either catalogue entries need a `convention_refs` field and validator coverage, or the D1.5 permission must be removed.

The ADR lists `track/items/review-scope-prompt-injection-2026-04-18/review.json` as an observed-data reference, while the same ADR later forbids ADRs and new tracks from directly referencing track-internal artifacts to preserve lifecycle boundaries. The document should either move/summarize that evidence into a track-crossing artifact or define an explicit exception for historical observation data.

D2.3 says canonical-block detection implementation details are in Q15, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields; Q14 is the canonical-block section. This broken reference points the T011/CLI implementation at the wrong design question.

D0.1 says any 🔴/🟡 SoT signal must trigger repair of the next upstream layer, but D3.1 and the /track:plan flow later define 🟡 as allowable and continue to the next phase. Implementers cannot satisfy both semantics: the orchestrator either loops on yellow or records a warning and proceeds.

The canonical-block warning bullet points implementers to Q15 for details, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields. The canonical-block unresolved decision is Q14, so this is a self-evidently wrong cross-reference.

D1.5 allows type catalog entries to reference conventions, but D3.2 says type catalogs have no convention reference field at all. D2.2 also lists `ConventionRef` use sites only for spec.json, so implementers cannot tell whether catalog `convention_refs` must exist and be verified or whether type-catalog-to-convention references are actually forbidden. The phase matrix still defines `Phase 3 完了` as `task-coverage.json` covering only all acceptance criteria and says merge is OK when SoT signals are blue. As written, this table can drive an implementation that permits a final merge with uncovered in-scope items or unresolved tasks.

This acceptance criterion is not satisfiable as written: it requires `informal_grounds[]` on type-catalogue entries to emit 🟡, but ADR D3.2 explicitly defers catalogue-side signal evaluation to a later ADR and only keeps the schema presence check in this scope.
